channel.title: NYT > Technology
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Meta Begins Laying Off 8,000 Employees Amid A.I. Transformation
item.description: Meta told employees last month that it would carry out mass layoffs on May 20, as the Silicon Valley giant tries to transform into an A.I.-first company.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan and Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta’s headquarters in Menlo Park, Calif. The company, which renamed itself for the metaverse, is transforming itself for the era of artificial intelligence.

item.title: How Google Is Starting to Win the A.I. Race
item.description: Despite its early stumbles, Google’s Gemini has leapfrogged ChatGPT in relevance and usefulness. Soon, it will be ubiquitous.
item.dc:creator: Brian X. Chen
item.media:credit: Sisi Yu

item.title: Powered by A.I., Google Changes Its Search Box for the First Time in 25 Years
item.description: Using a new Gemini A.I. model, the tech giant is overhauling its search box dimensions to answer longer queries, adding a video-generation tool and simplifying online shopping.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle, Kate Conger and Brian X. Chen
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: On Tuesday, Google said the A.I. shift had inspired it to overhaul its search bar for the first time since 2001.

item.title: As OpenAI Celebrates Court Win Against Musk, Other Challenges Lie Ahead
item.description: A jury’s rejection of Elon Musk’s $150 billion lawsuit against OpenAI was a major hurdle crossed. But the maker of ChatGPT faces a list of other problems.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo/Reuters
item.media:description: Lawyers for OpenAI, who won a resounding victory on Monday, outside the federal courthouse in Oakland, Calif.

item.title: Rising Energy Costs and Data Centers at Heart of NextEra’s Dominion Bid
item.description: NextEra’s proposed acquisition of Dominion Energy comes as Americans are paying a lot more for electricity, and data centers are demanding a lot more power from utilities.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Tristan Spinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: NextEra Energy’s acquisition of Dominion Energy would consolidate utility operations along much of the East Coast, which could shape electricity service for tens of millions of customers.

item.title: Senate Republicans Introduce Funding Measure to Combat Online Child Abuse
item.description: As part of the reconciliation bill, the senators have allocated $108 million over three years to add nearly 200 specialists at the Department of Homeland Security.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Josh Hawley, Republican of Missouri, has championed the reconciliation bill.

item.title: Tesla’s Semi Truck could Jolt the Trucking Industry
item.description: California truckers have expressed strong interest in the Tesla Semi because it costs much less and can travel further on a charge than electric trucks sold by established manufacturers.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Gabriela Bhaskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Screens on either side of a Tesla Semi’s steering wheel provide a view of the traffic around the vehicle.

item.title: Bipartisan Bill Would Impose New Annual Fee on Electric Vehicles
item.description: A House transportation bill introduced this week would require owners of electric cars to pay $130 to cover the cost of road repairs.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Congress is considering imposing a new annual fee on electric vehicles to help pay for road repairs.

item.title: After Elon Musk’s Court Loss Comes the Long Hot A.I. Summer
item.description: Even as protests increase, the collapse of Mr. Musk’s suit against OpenAI and Sam Altman will speed up the artificial intelligence juggernaut.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld and Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk on Monday lost his lawsuit accusing OpenAI and its chief executive, Sam Altman, of breaching the company’s founding contract.

item.title: Meta Reassigns 7,000 Employees to Focus on A.I.
item.description: The company announced the changes two days before it plans to lay off 10 percent of its work force, or about 8,000 employees.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Adrienne Grunwald for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new Meta Lab flagship store, which sells tech like smart glasses and virtual reality headsets, on Fifth Avenue in Manhattan.

item.title: Elon Musk Loses $150 Billion Suit Against OpenAI and Sam Altman
item.description: A nine-member jury found that Mr. Musk had waited too long to sue. The setback for the tech mogul frees OpenAI to continue in the artificial intelligence race.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: OpenAI’s president, Greg Brockman, right, was also named in Mr. Musk’s lawsuit.

item.title: Five Takeaways From the Blockbuster Trial Pitting Elon Musk Against OpenAI
item.description: It took a jury less than two hours to decide that Mr. Musk had waited too long to sue. But the testimony over three weeks was still illuminating.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal courthouse in Oakland, Calif., on Monday, when a jury decided that Elon Musk had waited too long to file his lawsuit against OpenAI.

item.title: What to Know About Elon Musk’s Trial Against OpenAI
item.description: A jury in Oakland, Calif., reached a decision after a three-week-long trial seen as pivotal for the future of OpenAI and the artificial intelligence race.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac

item.title: Here’s what was on the jury’s list for a decision.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Brennan Smart for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Ronald V. Dellums U.S. Courthouse in Oakland, Calif.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac

item.title: Peter G. Neumann, Who Warned of Computer Security Risks, Dies at 93
item.description: For decades, he criticized the industry’s lax attitudes toward computer security and individual digital privacy. He also developed solutions.
item.dc:creator: John Markoff
item.media:credit: Jim Wilson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Peter G. Neumann in 2012. At his death, he was still working full time on advanced computer security design being adopted by companies like Google and Microsoft.

item.title: Did You Mean to Share That Selfie? What to Know About Instagram Instants.
item.description: “Instants” blasts photos to your mutual followers or close friends, immediately. Here’s how to avoid accidental embarrassment.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler

item.title: OpenAI Bought Company That Offered A.I. Tools for Cloning Voices
item.description: The acquisition, Weights.gg, was a sort of social network for creating and sharing artificial intelligence algorithms.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Aaron Wojack for The New York Times
item.media:description: The San Francisco offices of OpenAI, which backed away from earlier efforts to clone voices with artificial intelligence.

item.title: A.I. Safety Is So Back + Mythos Mayhem with Nikesh Arora + Hot Mess Express
item.description: After several years of dismissing A.I. safety as doomer fear-mongering, parts of the Trump administration now seem ready to support regulation.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose, Casey Newton, Whitney Jones, Rachel Cohn, Vjeran Pavic, Chris Wood, Dan Powell, Elisheba Ittoop and Rowan Niemisto
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Photo: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: Nvidia’s Future in China Remains Unclear After Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: The standoff comes as Chinese firms increasingly turn to domestic chipmakers like Huawei, in a drive to reduce China’s dependence on Western technologies.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s chief executive, after a welcome ceremony at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: U.S. and China Will Start Discussing A.I. Safety, Bessent Says
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent did not say when these talks would happen. There are fears in the United States and China about the threats from A.I., but neither side is willing to slow down its development.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Wang
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent during the welcoming ceremony for President Trump in Beijing, on Thursday.

item.title: OpenAI Considers Legal Action Against Apple in Strained Relationship
item.description: The A.I. company, which is in the middle of a court fight with Elon Musk, has been unhappy with how Apple has integrated ChatGPT into its devices.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Apple Store in Grand Central Terminal in Manhattan. Apple struck a deal two years ago to support its devices with OpenAI’s ChatGPT chatbot.

item.title: OpenAI Trial Heads to Jury After Closing Arguments in Musk vs. Altman Case
item.description: Lawyers for Elon Musk and OpenAI made their closing arguments in the blockbuster federal trial on Thursday. Nine jurors are set to begin deliberations next week.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lawyers for Elon Musk and OpenAI made their closing arguments on Thursday in federal court in Oakland, Calif.

item.title: What the jury had to weigh to decide the case.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowd waits outside the Ronald V. Dellums U.S. Courthouse last month in Oakland, Calif.

item.title: Microsoft’s C.E.O. testified about the company’s OpenAI investments.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Satya Nadella, Microsoft’s chief executive, led his company’s investments into OpenAI.

item.title: Why A.I. Safety Controls Are Not Very Effective
item.description: Three years after the debut of ChatGPT, fooling A.I. systems into bad behavior is almost trivial.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Tiffany Hsu
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: A.I. systems from companies like OpenAI and Anthropic have guardrails that often prove to be porous.

item.title: Cerebras, A.I. Chip Maker, Rises 89% in Market Debut as Tech IPOs Ramp Up
item.description: Cerebras, a Silicon Valley maker of artificial intelligence chips, began trading on the stock market on Thursday, as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic also take steps to go public.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cerebras presages a series of potential “mega I.P.O.s” from A.I.-related firms including OpenAI, Anthropic and Elon Musk’s SpaceX.

item.title: Elon Musk’s lawyer asked Sam Altman on the stand: Are you trustworthy?
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive, spent the day on the witness stand in a federal court in Oakland, Calif., on Tuesday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac

item.title: Why ‘Smart’ Products Have Started to Look Like the Dumb Choice
item.description: How Wi-Fi-connecting, app-based tech led to a backlash in the name of simplicity.
item.dc:creator: Nitsuh Abebe

item.title: Ishmael Reed Is Writing a Play About Elon Musk
item.description: Ishmael Reed, provocateur and playwright, has a few words for the billionaires of Silicon Valley.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ishmael Reed, 88, an author who has frequently challenged American political culture, at his home in Oakland, Calif. He is writing a play about Elon Musk and other tech billionaires.

item.title: Crypto Industry Pushes a Bill to Tilt Regulation in Its Favor
item.description: After a series of political victories under President Trump, firms are lobbying Congress for a sweeping framework they helped shape.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Golden Cosmos

item.title: A Start-Up Aiming to Make Geothermal Energy Mainstream Goes Public
item.description: Fervo Energy, which uses drilling techniques from the oil and gas industry to produce power from the earth’s heat, raised $1.9 billion in an initial public offering.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Fervo Energy project near Milford, Utah. The company is trying to adapt drilling techniques from the oil and gas industry to unlock vast amounts of energy from the earth’s interior.

item.title: Dozens of Polymarket Bets Show Signs of Insider Trading, The Times Finds
item.description: Dozens of long-shot bets on Polymarket, from the war with Iran to the cryptocurrency market, have defied the odds, according to a New York Times examination.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Fuel Prices Drive Sales of E.V.s, Just Not in the U.S.
item.description: Electric vehicle sales have soared in Europe and much of the rest of the world, but Americans are still hesitant.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: High fuel prices are weighing on sales of vehicles that run on gasoline or diesel.

item.title: Silicon Valley’s A.I. Lobbying Blitz Reaches a Fever Pitch
item.description: OpenAI and Anthropic are opening offices in Washington, hiring lobbyists and spending more than ever to win over federal lawmakers.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Kylie Cooper/Reuters
item.media:description: OpenAI’s chief executive, Sam Altman, during a Washington conference on the nation’s infrastructure in March.

item.title: Notable Researchers Join $4 Billion Effort to Build Self-Improving A.I.
item.description: Recursive Superintelligence, founded by former Google, Meta and OpenAI researchers, is part of a growing effort to automate the creation of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Carolyn Fong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Five of Recursive Superintelligence’s eight founders, from left, Tim Shi, Yuandong Tian, Richard Socher, Caiming Xiong and Josh Tobin at the company’s office in San Francisco.

item.title: Tired of Hacked Passwords? Help Is on the Way.
item.description: Apps from Apple, Google and others can assist in making your online accounts more secure, even as new ways of logging in continue to take off.
item.dc:creator: J. D. Biersdorfer
item.media:credit: 1Password
item.media:description: A password manager, like the 1Password app shown here, serves as a secure vault for all your user names, passwords and other private information.

item.title: Andreessen Horowitz Is Playing Politics Like No Other
item.description: “If you think there’s a lot of money in politics now,” Marc Andreessen said in 2000, “you haven’t seen anything yet.” His firm is now the biggest known spender on this campaign cycle.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Ashley Cai
item.media:credit: Ashley Cai/The New York Times
